unit.name: Fine-tuning pretrained encoders and lightweight adaptation
unit.description: Shows how encoder representations are turned into downstream predictions, when BERT is appropriate, and how RoBERTa and parameter-efficient tuning refine the recipe.
unit.summary: After pretraining, an encoder yields one contextual vector per input token; to solve a downstream task, the pretraining prediction head is removed and a task-specific classifier is attached to one or more hidden states, then the model is fine-tuned end to end [ts=3056s] [ts=3133s]. The segment notes BERT model sizes and emphasizes the practical separation between expensive pretraining and relatively cheap downstream fine-tuning, which can often be done on modest hardware [ts=3141s] [ts=3189s]. It also clarifies where BERT fits best: because its objective is fill-in-the-blank reconstruction rather than left-to-right generation, it is strong for document or sentence representation tasks such as classification, but less natural for free-form text generation [ts=3211s] [ts=3247s]. Several improvements are then sketched: span masking can be harder and more useful than masking isolated subwords, and RoBERTa improves on BERT by dropping next-sentence prediction and training on more data [ts=3253s] [ts=3319s]. The lecture closes with parameter-efficient fine-tuning ideas such as prefix tuning, prompt tuning, and low-rank weight updates, all motivated by preserving strong pretrained weights while adapting cheaply to a new task [ts=3353s] [ts=3497s].

key_points.text: For downstream tasks, remove the masked-token prediction head and add a task-specific classifier on top of encoder states.
BERT is better suited to classification and representation tasks than to open-ended generation.
Masking whole spans can be harder and more effective than masking isolated subwords.
RoBERTa improves BERT mainly by removing next-sentence prediction and training on more text.
Prefix tuning freezes the pretrained network and learns a small set of prepended pseudo-token parameters instead.
Low-rank adaptation learns a small low-rank update added to frozen weight matrices.

The segment notes BERT model sizes and emphasizes the practical separation between expensive pretraining and relatively cheap downstream fine-tuning, which can often be done on modest hardware [ts=3141s] [ts=3189s]. It also clarifies where BERT fits best: because its objective is fill-in-the-blank reconstruction rather than left-to-right generation, it is strong for document or sentence representation tasks such as classification, but less natural for free-form text generation [ts=3211s] [ts=3247s]. Several improvements are then sketched: span masking can be harder and more useful than masking isolated subwords, and RoBERTa improves on BERT by dropping next-sentence prediction and training on more data [ts=3253s] [ts=3319s]. kp_catalog.name: BERT and encoder transfer
kp_catalog.description: Using a pretrained bidirectional encoder such as BERT for downstream tasks by attaching lightweight prediction heads and fine-tuning.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because bERT and encoder transfer clarifies using a pretrained bidirectional encoder such as BERT for downstream tasks by attaching lightweight prediction heads and fine-tuning, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning
kp_catalog.description: Fine-tuning approach that updates only a small subset of model parameters to reduce memory, storage, and sometimes overfitting risk. Adapting pretrained models by changing only a small number of parameters, such as prefixes, prompts, or low-rank weight updates.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because parameter-efficient fine-tuning formalizes how fine-tuning approach that updates only a small subset of model parameters to reduce memory, storage, and sometimes overfitting risk, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between high and medium.

kp_catalog.name: Masked language modeling
kp_catalog.description: Corrupting input text and training an encoder to reconstruct masked tokens using bidirectional context.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because masked language modeling clarifies corrupting input text and training an encoder to reconstruct masked tokens using bidirectional context, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
